figures.caption: Figure 1: Cross-validation of the ADI and PINN-PI solutions on a 200 × 200 grid at t = 0. Top row: value function Φ(v,X) from ADI (left) and PINN-PI (center); relative error (ΦADI − ΦPINN)/ΦADI in percent (right). Bottom row: optimal portfolio ω∗(v,X) from ADI (left) and PINN-PI (center); absolute error (right).
Table 2: Parameter values used in the numerical analysis. Values are taken from the literature rather than calibrated to a specific dataset.
Table 7: Structural comparison of the two numerical methods.
Table 1: Behavioral anomalies generated by asymmetric nonlinear extrapolation.

Figure 4: Directional response gap ∆(x0 = 0.3; v, β) against β ∈ [0, 1] at three variance levels. The slope at β = 0 is positive at each v. At low v, ∆ is positive over the whole β range; at higher v the 1/v scaling suppresses the myopic term and the curve can turn non-monotone in β.

Figure 5: Squared-turnover channels on the (v,X) plane at the baseline. Left: sentiment channel IX = (ω∗ X)2α2v. Middle: variance channel Iv = (ω∗ v) 2σ2 vv. Right: relative magnitude IX/Iv (clipped at 5). IX stays bounded away from zero on the interior, so the hedging components of ω∗ X do not cancel the positive myopic term (20). The dominance of Iv in the low-v layer is a Heston property of ω∗ v , not an extrapolation effect.

Table 5: Welfare loss (%) under alternative parameter values. The baseline corresponds to (θ, λ, β) = (0.15, 3.0, 0.5). For each panel, “Linear” reports the loss under the linear-extrapolation benchmark of Proposition 3, “Asymmetric” the loss under the asymmetric nonlinear policy of this paper, and “Gap” the saturation benefit (Linear − Asymmetric).

Figure 3: Welfare loss (%, relative to the rational benchmark) as a function of θ (left), λ (center), and β (right). One parameter is varied at a time; the others are held at their baseline values from Table 2.
Table 3: Discrepancy between ADI and PINN-PI on the 200×200 grid at t = 0. The economically relevant region is defined as v > 0.01, corresponding to annualized volatility above 10%.

Figure 2: Decomposition of the optimal portfolio into its three components. Top row: dependence on the sentiment X at three variance levels. Bottom row: dependence on the variance v at three sentiment levels.
